speaker: interviewer
text: May 25, 1995. The survivor is Lola Kay. This is tape five. All right, let's continue now with the woman, this Mrs. Rockefeller, that you were talking about.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name wasn't--

speaker: interviewer
text: I know--

speaker: interviewee
text: They were rich. They were known something like that in the city. So she saw me taking out those kettles-- which I didn't even-- you know I was just propping up, make believe that I do. She said, oh, if I can give you a piece of bread. And she was, you know, she was ready to drop-- a bag of bones-- you've never seen anything. She used to-- she was-- you know-- In Poland, when you're rich, you have a chance to go to different countries. I think she was studying in Egypt or something. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Anyway, she you decided to help her--

speaker: interviewee
text: She decided that--

speaker: interviewer
text: You decided to help her.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm going to go in to take a piece of bread--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: The breads were out, you know, cut into rations. then, little did I know that across the street was another kitchen, and the chef, the SS man, he spotted me-- that I gave something. He came, right away, and he told the-- you know, that kapo-- she was from Lodz, girl-- beautiful girl. And he told her that I'm--

speaker: interviewer
text: You're finished.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm going to be hanged. What I did-- I'm going to be hanged. She went furious, she went. [SIGHS] I said, God Almighty, all I did is gave her piece of bread. That was my piece of bread. I had that piece of bread that I steal from the kitchen-- and I-- you know, that was a crime, and for that you are-- you're going to be hung. And she started to negotiate. [SIGHS] She calmed me down. She said, I'll do the best I can. And she risked her life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she saved you.

speaker: interviewee
text: She saved me. Whatever she did, should God forgive her. If she ever is there, I wish her all the best. Her name was Paula, and I love her for that.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never knew what happened to Paula?

speaker: interviewee
text: I never knew.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, maybe she survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, she did, whatever she did that night, she did a good thing. She saved my life.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, now you got into Block 29. Let's find out what happened in Block 29.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. [SIGHS] After that-- cause I had this severe, you know, thing with my feet-- I went on Block 29. And it was crowded-- the people were dying of-- of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Typhus?

speaker: interviewee
text: Dysentery.

speaker: interviewer
text: Dysentery.

speaker: interviewee
text: The typhus-- they just-- it was just unbelievable. They just turn black, and then they would go and-- dead on top of the dead. There were mountains of dead. And then I couldn't help noticing this. I didn't know about Anne Frank until, you know, here, in the States.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I didn't know her from-- from-- I came from Poland, and this one came from--

speaker: interviewer
text: From Holland.

speaker: interviewee
text: From Holland. So she was noticeable because she was [NON-ENGLISH], like. You know-- going around, she didn't-- she took the clothes or was with bugs, or lice or things, and she scratched it so bad. She had lesions, a bag of bones-- there was nothing on her. And she was crying all the time. She wanted her mother. Her mother was taken away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Her sister was with her at the time, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her sister died. She was on the bottom. She was in the middle. Her sister fell off. She was dead. Who knew that that was Anne Frank's sister? And I think she had some help from-- from somebody outside. They gave her some-- she wouldn't touch any food. She wouldn't eat nothing. She just had that blanket wrapped around-- a dirty blanket, a gray blanket, around her body, nude, nothing on her. And all she did is cry and scream and cry constantly.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did this last?

speaker: interviewee
text: It didn't last. I was-- you know-- [SIGHS] I was to the end. It was almost the end. And then--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was near the end of the war-- this was--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, end.

speaker: interviewer
text: April of '45?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was April. That was April. Then we had the men's camp. You know, over the fence, there was a men's camp, separate. So they-- we used to take a blink, you know? There was nothing to see. What I saw is Russian prisoners. The prisoners were so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Starved?

speaker: interviewee
text: Starved, that they could-- one foot after the other, they dragged one foot after the other. They opened-- the-- the-- you know, if somebody died, they cut the stomach and then took the livers out. They had a fire.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they practiced cannibalism.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you--

speaker: interviewee
text: I watched and I couldn't believe.

speaker: interviewer
text: You watched this?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I reminded myself today, I just couldn't get it out of my mind. I felt like throwing up. [SIGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: So-- You were finally liberated, then.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was something in the men's camp, so I think a white flag. That was midnight, like 12 o'clock, midnight, something. We heard something. Then, I knew that the water was-- was poisoned.

speaker: interviewer
text: The water was poisoned?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, that's why so many people died.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you mean the Nazis poisoned--

speaker: interviewee
text: The reason--

speaker: interviewer
text: --poisoned the water--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, when they left, they all left.

speaker: interviewer
text: They all left.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was nobody in charge anymore. They all left.

speaker: interviewer
text: And they poisoned the water.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were on our own.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't know it.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I didn't touch the-- the reason that I'm here is I was careful not to eat any garbage, like they did-- scraps, you know, things like that-- and no water. I'm not a drinker of water, you know, even today.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I didn't touch anything. The water was-- was poisoned. They left, there was no Nazis, nobody to take care. On our own, and that's why. So that was midnight. So we heard-- we heard something on the loud speakers. And then we heard noises. I think they cut the electric wires-- the men already did. And they started to come in-- in the-- in the women's-- Section.

speaker: interviewee
text: Section. And I didn't know what's going on. I had no idea that that's the end, and I couldn't believe it. Until this day, I don't believe it, like I'm sitting and talking to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the British-- the British came--

speaker: interviewee
text: The British came. That was-- you know-- that was in the afternoon. They started moving in-- with water. And there were people, you know, begging to be helped.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: And whatever they could, they did. But they were just falling like flies.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you realize you were liberated? When did the realization hit you? Or didn't it?

speaker: interviewee
text: When I saw the British army-- when I saw the British. And then, of course, you see that we're liberated. But I couldn't believe it, that I'm here, alive, that I survived. Everybody's-- mountains of dead, you know, like-- like buildings. The smell was so bad that you couldn't stand it. And then whoever was around me is dead. Whoever I knew is dead. And I couldn't I couldn't believe so I still didn't believe that I survived.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I-- I said, everybody, you know, all those people-- still, we're liberated, and there's so many dying? [SIGHS] I said, I didn't want to go to-- they started to take, you know, the sick ones to the nearby hospital so we're taken care. That, I didn't want to do. You also told me was a tremendous tragedy. That the British saw all these starving people, and they did something that was a great tragedy. Do you remember?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, my God. I missed this out? I have to tell you that. That was in Bergen-- in Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I was sick. They selected 1,000 very sick women, and they took them to-- they were supposed to go to Sweden.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was selected. And we went there. We arrived. They wanted us to-- first to cooperate-- you know, to recuperate a little, to get a little more strength. So they kept us for a couple of weeks. But there was not enough food. The ones who were stronger, they went out, hunting for-- you know, like, there were farmers growing vegetables, I think, to survive. I couldn't do anything. Until, you know, one man-- an older man, saw, and he went-- I remember, he went out-- there was a Polack. He went out and he caught a chicken. From a farmer, he caught a chicken. And he cooked the chicken. I don't know where. And he gave me some of that soup.

speaker: interviewer
text: So the British didn't give you enough food. There you're liberated, but they didn't give you enough food.

speaker: interviewee
text: But then, I find out that a friend of mine-- we were together in Birkenau, I knew her. [NON-ENGLISH], she was from [NON-ENGLISH]. She helped me-- you know, she told me to hold on and-- you know-- when I was so weak.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: She said, keep going. Don't give up. And I saw she was working in the-- in the kitchen that brought milk from Holland, because we were, then, right on the Holland border-- in Lingen. It was right on the Holland border. And there was milk-- they used to deliver milk in that kitchen. And she worked there. Somehow, you know, she was very, very clever and very-- you know, she wasn't sick enough. So she worked. I came in and she used a scoop off from the-- from those cans, the milk came in, from the top.

speaker: interviewer
text: The cream.

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- and she gave me to drink. Believe it or not, what a change in my wounds. They were trying to heal like never before-- the wounds. And we were there about three weeks to recuperate. And I felt-- I felt better. Then, it was time to go to Sweden.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: They-- you know, the soldiers were ready to take us, the trucks, everything. And they were going among themselves, you know, the girls, the women, they said they're not going to go. They might, you know, they're going to put them in a sanatorium, or what. And, then, they're going to miss out-- maybe somebody still alive from their family, or Israel, or these States, or God knows what. And they're going to wind up in a sanatorium. And they didn't want to do that. So, in the middle of the night, everybody disappeared. They were hiding in the fields-- in the open fields. They stayed overnight. And when the trucks came to take us, there was nobody there to go.

speaker: interviewer
text: So, eventually you went back to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewee
text: So whoever was left there, we went all back to Bergen-Belsen.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, tell me how you met--

speaker: interviewee
text: They couldn't find us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me how you met your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. In Bergen-Belsen, we wind up in better quarters-- where the SS, and-- in concrete houses.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know already-- not in those shacks. They had-- you know-- that place was closed up, I think. And we went up on the fields for-- I winded up there. And I always had somebody, I was so lucky, to take me under their wings.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went up. We were one, two, three, four in one room. It was a small room, but there were bunk beds, four, you know. And this little woman, she survived. And she worked in the kitchen, I think. The British didn't give you to eat. No?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: I thought they did.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were hungry, so hungry, you wouldn't believe it. So she worked in the kitchen. She used to bring a potato, or something, you know, for me. And, of course, they gave you already from the UNRRA--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, supplies--

speaker: interviewee
text: -The old-- no, no supplies--

speaker: interviewer
text: Not supplies?

speaker: interviewee
text: Old clothes that people donated. So, when I met a friend from Warsaw, she also took me under her wings, and she sewed something for me. A skirt-- she made me a skirt and a jacket. She made me some clothes.

speaker: interviewer
text: You still didn't tell me how you met you husband. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I get to there.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're getting there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Uh-huh.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I wore clothes, nice. That she-- she was one of the-- she sew-- she was a seamstress for actors in Warsaw, for, you know, for the theater, you know? Very fashionable things. So they-- things are getting, you know, to be-- people are meeting families they find already, maybe a sister or brother or what. I have nobody, and I have no prospect to meet anybody because I knew that everybody was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Except your sister. But you didn't know that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Didn't know my sister. I didn't know where she was, though she wasn't too far from where I was in Bitterfeld. She was in Mauthausen. And that wasn't too far. It was about 20 kilometers or something. But that Kitty Hart-- she was a tomboy. She was only 12 years old. She took-- went on a bike, and she went there on a bike.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she found your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: Her mother was furious. And she saw my sister. And she came and she said, Kotek, she used to call me because that's what--

speaker: interviewer
text: Kotek is aunt.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was my-- Kotek is cat.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, but it means what? What does it mean?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my name.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, Kotek.

speaker: interviewee
text: I picked a short, little name that I remember and when I--

speaker: interviewer
text: I thought kotek meant aunt in Polish. All right.

speaker: interviewee
text: Kotek is a kitten.

speaker: interviewer
text: A kitten, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I wished-- I wished all the time that I was an ant-- that I was a bird, I was anything but not a Jew--

speaker: interviewer
text: Not an ant, no. An aunt or an uncle, I didn't mean an ant.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, a aunt or an uncle. I didn't expect that-- in the States we had no family. Nobody ever emigrated.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me how you met your husband.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what you're interested?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah I just, I asked the question--

speaker: interviewee
text: So, in-- in Birkenau-- in Bergen-Belsen we walked.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had this-- we were three from, we were together. And one was from Landsberg, I think. She came to Bergen-Belsen, also, with her brother, to look. I was liberated with her sister's fiance. They were going before the war-- [NON-ENGLISH], I was liberated with him. He was an inspiration to everybody. Oh--I have pictures of him.

speaker: interviewer
text: So she came and they found each other. And he survived, and his sister survived. And [NON-ENGLISH] survived. So everybody winded up in Bergen-Belsen, and we were working in Bergen-Belsen. And then we saw a bunch of guys out walking. And they asked directions where to the office to look up books, if they're any-- you know-- maybe they're going to find some-- Survivors?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some names or what. So we directed them. Sure enough, this one of them was looking for a date. That was my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: You finally got to him, OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: No, he didn't get to me. So he asked if he can come the next day, if they want to go out-- to go out for a walk or what. So I was the youngest among the three. They at-- looked at him. They figured he is too young, maybe, for them. They don't want to go out. They said, you're the one who's going to go out. I said no, I'm not. I never went on a-- I don't know what a date is-- never went out on a date. I don't go no place. Anyway, finally, they convinced me-- he's not going to harm you. There's nothing he can do to you.

speaker: interviewer
text: He's a nice Jewish boy.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if he was Jew-- he spoke Polish--

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, you didn't know if he was Jewish or not.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know. Oh, he-- I knew he was probably Jewish. But what he did is spoke a-- a very fine, you know, intelligentsia, is what you would call that. Cause he said his own verse on that and that.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he came, and I risked. I went. You risked.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: You took a chance--

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know what a date is. I went.

speaker: interviewer
text: And?

speaker: interviewee
text: And we walked around. So he started talking about, you know, somewhere we were from, when and everything he told me. And he spoke, I-- I knew that he knew a lot. He was very intelligent, and very-- you know-- I liked, you know, what he said.

speaker: interviewer
text: First date.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. But I didn't thought anything about it, I mean, you know, that was not even in my mind. But the Polish language he used was intelligentsia, you know, educated boy. Not that I'm the maven, but I knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, in the meantime, I had somebody else who was really interested in me. He was a dentist in Bergen-Belsen. And he came from-- he was from Krakow. And he came to Bergen-Belsen. He kind of got away from Poland, everybody winds up in Bergen-Belsen because that was the women's-- you know-- Camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Camp, liberated, so. And he stayed in the-- in the same block. You know, it was a nice-- the SS used to live there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So this man tried to come on to me, and he was much older than I am. He was a dentist, you know-- then, so I figure.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I knew he was coming on. He used to go and-- and-- bring-- nobody-- I don't know where he got flowers-- bring flowers, all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Made you feel good.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And he used to take walks with me. And I took walks. But he was serious.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh.

speaker: interviewee
text: He started to be serious. Then he told me--

speaker: interviewer
text: That scared you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Hm?

speaker: interviewer
text: Did that scare you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I didn't know anything about a man or what. I mean, I don't know where I was. I still didn't even believe that I'm free.

speaker: interviewee
text: Where I'm going to go? Where do you go? I have nobody. I don't know nobody. No place-- where you wind up? So, he said, he's a dentist, he's got to go back to Poland to liquidate his office. The equipment, that's to sell, or what, and he's moving. He wanted me to go with him. He didn't want to leave me there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he told the woman, the what she gave out the rations and everything in that block, you know, all the people were living, to keep an eye on me and everything. Finally, he said, when he met my husband, OK, he said, don't you dare ever talk to this-- you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: This girl.

speaker: interviewee
text: No-- no, to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: I shouldn't talk to him. He said, he's not for real, he's just that and that. He said he didn't agree anything, anything my husband said. He disagreed.

speaker: interviewee
text: He was a-- you know-- a settled, a dentist, a mature person. And he was only-- how old was my husband? He's only two years older than I am. So he's 22.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So, you know-- and then he lived through the war and everything. I mean, he didn't mature, yet. I mean, you know. So-- he said-- And he left. I remember, like now, that he just grabbed me, this Herman-- I have pictures of him-- to go with him. He said we're going to go, and he was going to liquidate. And he wouldn't leave me because he knew that he was going to leave me with George.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, OK. He left you with George.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I said, he's gone, goodbye.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, he's going to keep in touch. And he kept in touch. After he sold everything there, I heard that he liquidated all his things. But I was already in-- oh, I went-- I went with my husband. You know--

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to a displaced--

speaker: interviewee
text: There were four-- yeah-- we went-- I had to get out from Bergen-Belsen because there was no food, no future, and the winter was coming. And I had no-- you know--

speaker: interviewer
text: Right so you went--

speaker: interviewee
text: I couldn't survive.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to another DP camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I went-- They were already established in Bavaria, in Schwandorf.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: His brother-- he was with his brother and three more friends. Michael, and Abraham, and one more-- one was also my age. So I went with him, under the condition-- I'm going to find, you know, from the UNRRA-- you know, they give you-- whatever. And I went to ORT to learn a trade. I wanted, still, to go to Israel. I have pictures of that. I am a life member of ORT.

speaker: interviewer
text: So and, to school-- I went to school in Schwandorf, and I wanted to go to Israel. So, then, I found-- there came a-- a letter to the Jewish Center in Schwandorf that the rabbi that so-and-so [NON-ENGLISH] is looking for a sister that old and that old. Because, when we-- when I was with my husband, going already, in the American zone in Landsberg there, he recognized me.

speaker: interviewee
text: He told me that he saw my sister alive. He told me that my sister's alive. That, I knew, but I didn't know where and when. When that letter came to that rabbi-- that-- you know-- so I had already contacted then. I figured, dear God, I have somebody who's alive. Because I didn't know where I'm going to wind up, still. I didn't know-- want to wind up with-- with this. To me, I was still a displaced person, and I had nothing and nobody. And I don't know what. All I wanted to go is to Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened between--

speaker: interviewee
text: That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah, between 1945 and 1949, before you came to United States, what happened in those four years?

speaker: interviewee
text: In those four years, we were living in Schwandorf, and life was tough. Because-- oh, and then I find my sister. I'll just tell you.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went. I had to go there. She was living in Lampertheim near Frankfurt, in Hessen, near Heidelberg, you know. Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: Near Mannheim. It was nice. It was just a warmer climate there. And in Bavaria it was cold, but beautiful. So I picked myself up, and he wouldn't let me go by myself. I wanted to run away. I said, I find a sister, I can't-- and I knew that she can do things. And she is probably-- whatever she did, I would-- you know-- I woudln't-- I would have help.

speaker: interviewer
text: You wouldn't have to worry, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So, and we traveled by train. He wouldn't let me go by myself.

speaker: interviewer
text: He went with you.

speaker: interviewee
text: He forced me that he should go with me.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, we'll--

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll continue with your sister in a minute.

speaker: interviewee
text: We have to stop? OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I scratch my eye? It's itching.
